common_name: Rattlesnake Pole Bean (Purple-Streaked Heirloom Pole Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; Rattlesnake is a vigorous pole bean that needs a sturdy 6-foot trellis or pole support. In zone 9, plant March–May for summer harvest; a late summer planting in August–September can produce well in the cooler fall. Rattlesnake shows excellent heat and drought tolerance, making it one of the most reliable pole beans for the Gulf South summer. The pods turn fully green at maturity but are streaked with purple while developing.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost; install a 6-foot trellis or pole support before or at planting. Harvest pods when 5–7 inches long and pods are still tender — before seeds inside begin to bulge prominently. Rattlesnake (also known as Preacher Bean or Streaked Cranberry) is a Southern heirloom pole bean with a documented history stretching back at least 200 years in Appalachian and Gulf South gardens, where its combination of heat tolerance, productivity, and exceptional flavor made it a staple of traditional kitchen gardens. The name comes from the pods' appearance: pale to medium green pods with distinctive purple-brown streaks and mottling that resemble rattlesnake skin — a striking visual character that makes the variety easy to identify in the garden and interesting as a fresh or dried bean. The flavor is particularly noteworthy — Rattlesnake has a richer, fuller, more complex bean flavor than most modern bush bean varieties, with a slightly nutty, almost buttery quality when cooked as a snap bean. The beans also mature into excellent shell beans (harvested when seeds are plump but before pods dry) and dry beans (harvested when pods are fully dry and papery), making Rattlesnake a versatile three-stage bean. The pole habit — 5–7 feet tall — requires support but produces over a longer season than bush types and handles the heat and drought of Gulf South summers better than most bush bean varieties.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Rattlesnake pole bean variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Southern heirloom bean collection
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Pole Bean Production in the Southeast

common_name: Contender Bush Bean (Heat-Tolerant Heirloom Bush Snap Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Contender is one of the most heat-tolerant bush bean varieties and can be succession-planted every 2–3 weeks for continuous harvest. In zone 9, plant March–May for summer production and again August–September for fall harvest. Contender's early maturity (48–56 days) makes it one of the fastest bush beans to produce — an advantage in zone 9 where summer heat can shut down bean production.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1–1.5 inches deep in rows 18–24 inches apart; thin to 3–6 inch spacing. Harvest pods when 4–6 inches long and pencil-width, before seeds swell. Contender is a 1950s-era open-pollinated bush bean developed specifically for heat tolerance and productivity in the eastern United States — one of the few traditional snap bean varieties bred with the hot, humid summers of the South in mind rather than the cooler climates where most American snap bean varieties perform best. The historical record shows Contender was developed at the Tennessee Agricultural Experiment Station (now University of Tennessee) during the mid-20th century, specifically targeting the challenging conditions of southeastern summer gardens. Contender produces straight, medium-length (5–6 inch), round-podded pods of a medium-dark green color with a mild, clean snap bean flavor. The plants are compact and bushy (18–24 inches), producing a well-concentrated set of pods suitable for a single-harvest or two-harvest approach. The combination of heat tolerance, early maturity, and reliable productivity explains why Contender remains one of the most widely recommended snap beans for the South after more than 70 years — it is consistently listed by southern cooperative extension services as among the best-adapted bush beans for the region.
sources: University of Tennessee Agricultural Extension — Contender bean variety notes
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Heat-tolerant bean variety comparison
North Carolina State University Extension — Snap Bean Variety Recommendations

common_name: Royalty Purple Pod Bean (AAS-Winning Purple Bush Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Royalty Purple Pod germinates well in cooler soil than most snap beans (down to 55°F) — an advantage for early planting in zones 5–7 where it is often planted 2 weeks before the safe date for other beans. In zone 9, plant March–May or August–September. The purple pod color allows easy pod detection during harvest — the purple pods stand out visually against the dark green foliage, making picking faster and more complete than with green-podded varieties.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1–1.5 inches deep after last frost; harvest pods when 5–6 inches long, while still tender and before the purple color begins to fade. Royalty Purple Pod is a 1976 All-America Selections-winning bush bean known for its striking deep purple pods — an unusual color in the snap bean world and a practical advantage because the vivid pods are easy to see against green foliage during harvest. The purple color results from anthocyanin pigments that disappear completely when cooked, leaving behind the standard green of cooked snap beans — a fact that surprises first-time growers who expect to retain the purple color. The flavor of Royalty is notably good — richer and slightly more complex than many modern bush bean hybrids — and the plants show slightly better cool-soil germination tolerance than green-podded snap beans. Royalty Purple Pod has maintained its status as a respected open-pollinated variety for nearly 50 years since its AAS award, used both as a practical garden bean and as a visual accent in edible landscapes where the purple-podded plants contrast attractively with green foliage.
sources: All-America Selections — Royalty Purple Pod bean AAS winner 1976
Seed Savers Exchange — Purple pod bean collection
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Snap bean variety comparison

common_name: Gold Rush Yellow Wax Bean (AAS-Winning Straightpod Wax Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; succession plant every 2–3 weeks for continuous yellow wax bean harvest. Gold Rush produces a well-concentrated pod set ideal for once-over harvest or a 1–2 week intensive harvest window. In zone 9, plant March–May and again August–September for fall production.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1–1.5 inches deep in rows 18–24 inches apart; harvest pods when 4–6 inches long and still tender. Gold Rush is a 1980 All-America Selections-winning yellow wax bush bean that earned recognition for producing exceptionally straight, slim, uniformly golden-yellow pods in a well-concentrated set ideal for both fresh market and home canning. Yellow wax beans are a distinct culinary category from green snap beans — the pale golden-yellow color (which remains after cooking), slightly creamier texture, and subtle flavor difference (milder, slightly less grassy than green beans) make wax beans a favorite in German and Eastern European cooking and preferred by many home gardeners for pickled beans and canned beans. Gold Rush produces pods of exceptional straightness and uniformity — the 5–6 inch pods are nearly pencil-straight, a quality attribute for both visual appeal at the table and ease of packing for canning. The plants are compact, upright, and productive, concentrating the pod set over a 7–10 day harvest window suitable for once-over mechanical harvest or home garden batch picking.
sources: All-America Selections — Gold Rush bean AAS winner 1980
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Yellow wax bean variety comparison
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Snap and Wax Bean Production

common_name: Cherokee Wax Bush Bean (Heirloom Yellow Wax Snap Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Cherokee Wax is an open-pollinated heirloom that has been maintained for generations as a reliable yellow wax bean for home garden production. In zone 9, plant March–May and again in late August–September for fall production. The open-pollinated nature allows seed-saving from year to year — select the straightest, most productive plants for the best maintained seed stock.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1–1.5 inches deep in rows 18–24 inches apart after last frost; harvest when pods are 5–6 inches long and bright golden-yellow before pods begin to become stringy. Cherokee Wax is a traditional open-pollinated yellow wax bush bean that has been grown in American gardens since at least the early 20th century — one of the oldest continuously maintained wax bean varieties in American seed commerce. Unlike modern hybrid wax beans selected for mechanical harvest uniformity, Cherokee Wax represents the traditional wax bean type: productive, reliable, with good flavor, and completely open-pollinated so seed can be saved from season to season. The pods are bright golden-yellow, fairly straight, moderately thick-podded, with the characteristic waxy skin that gives the wax bean type its name. The eating quality is excellent — the thick walls provide a satisfying snap and the mild, slightly sweet wax bean flavor is cleaner and lighter than green bean types. Cherokee Wax has a loyal following among seed-saving gardeners and those who prefer open-pollinated varieties for independence from commercial seed sources.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Cherokee Wax bean variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Heirloom wax bean collection
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Traditional wax bean varieties
